ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03311737); no specific lychee-flavoured low-fat variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers tea-based beverages with milk. No specific lychee milk tea or low-fat variant code exists in FoodEx2; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226465004 represents milk tea as a substance class; no lychee-specific or low-fat variant code exists. Closest parent used.

preparation_method: flavoured with lychee syrup or lychee fruit extract
served chilled or at room temperature
main_ingredients: lychee syrup or lychee fruit flavouring

serving.natural_language_portion: One large (L) cup of approximately 500 ml, as served at KOI Thé and similar Taiwanese bubble-tea chains in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Milk Tea, prepared with low-fat milk and flavoured syrup — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for brewed black tea, low-fat milk (1% milkfat), and lychee-flavoured syrup, combined in proportions typical of a Taiwanese-style milk tea beverage
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for lychee milk tea with low-fat milk exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data: brewed black tea (FDC ID 173228), low-fat milk 1% milkfat (FDC ID 173354), and lychee-flavoured sugar syrup approximated from USDA generic sugar syrup data. Assumed volumetric composition: approximately 50% brewed tea, 35% low-fat milk, 15% lychee syrup by volume. Per-100 g values derived from this weighted blend. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweetened milk tea estimated from analogous foods: sweetened tea with milk (GI ~55) and lychee-flavoured sugar syrup contribution, based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for tea-based sweetened beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lychee milk tea with low-fat milk. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened milk tea and similar tea-based beverages with added sugar. The lychee syrup component contributes sucrose-equivalent sugars (GI ~65), while the milk component lowers the overall GI. A blended GI of approximately 55 (low category) is defensible. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 49.0) / 100 = 26.95, rounded to 26.9. Serving carbohydrate = 9.8 g per 100 g × 500 g serving = 49.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content at approximately 22 mg per 100 g (110 mg per large serving)
hypertension.guidance: The low sodium content of this beverage poses minimal direct risk for hypertension; however, individuals should account for total daily sugar intake, as high sugar consumption can indirectly elevate blood pressure over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (approximately 9.2 g sugar per 100 g; ~46 g per large serving) and low glycemic index of 55
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid this beverage due to its high free-sugar load (~46 g per large serving) and low GI of 55, which can still cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; requesting reduced or no sugar at point of order is strongly advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar content contributing to excess caloric intake and potential dyslipidaemia risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sweetened milk tea beverages with high added sugar content is associated with elevated triglyceride levels and increased cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake and opt for unsweetened or reduced-sugar versions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Low Fat Lychee Milk Tea (Large) — known in Chinese as 大杯低脂荔枝奶茶 — is a Taiwanese-style flavoured milk tea beverage sold in Singapore by KOI Thé and similar bubble-tea chains. It is prepared by combining brewed black tea or oolong tea with low-fat milk and lychee-flavoured syrup, resulting in a lightly sweet, floral, and mildly creamy drink. The lychee flavour imparts a distinctive tropical-fruit aroma and sweetness, while the low-fat milk base provides a lighter mouthfeel compared to full-fat or creamer-based milk teas. The beverage is typically served chilled over ice in a large (L) cup of approximately 500 ml. Nutritionally, it provides approximately 48 kcal per 100 g, with the caloric content driven primarily by added sugars (~9.2 g per 100 g) from the lychee syrup and any additional sweetener. Protein content is modest at ~1.2 g per 100 g from the low-fat milk component, and fat is low at ~0.5 g per 100 g. The large serving delivers approximately 240 kcal and 46 g of free sugars, making it a high-sugar beverage. Clinically, it is of high concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its significant free-sugar load and low glycemic index (~55), and of moderate concern for cardiovascular health due to the sugar-driven caloric density. Sodium is low (~110 mg per large serving), posing minimal hypertension risk from sodium alone. The beverage contains milk and is therefore not suitable for those with dairy allergies or lactose intolerance. It is vegetarian and halal-friendly.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for lychee milk tea with low-fat milk exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central branded databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for brewed black tea, low-fat milk, and generic sugar syrup in proportions typical of a Taiwanese-style milk tea. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened milk tea. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data (brewed black tea FDC ID 173228, low-fat milk 1% milkfat FDC ID 173354, generic sugar syrup) blended in approximate proportions of 50% tea, 35% low-fat milk, 15% lychee syrup by volume. Actual values will vary by recipe, sugar level customisation, and preparation at individual KOI Thé outlets. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for the component ingredients at the blend level used here. 3. vitamin_e_mg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source data for this beverage blend. 4. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source data for this beverage blend. 5. polyunsaturated_fat_g and trans_fat_g are 0.0; these are confirmed zero or effectively zero by formulation given the low-fat milk and tea base — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy component data. 6. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as brewed tea and low-fat milk contain no dietary fibre, and lychee syrup contributes negligible fibre — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. 7. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened milk tea; no direct GI measurement for lychee milk tea with low-fat milk is available. 8. Glycemic load per large serving (500 g): (55 × 49.0 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 26.9. 9. This record covers the large (L) size as sold at KOI Thé Singapore. Smaller sizes will have proportionally lower nutrient totals per serving. 10. The food is served by 1 restaurant (KOI Thé — Funan) on field survey data at SGD 6.80.
